title: Lowest Common Ancestor (BST)
code: public TreeNode LowestCommonAncestorBST(TreeNode root, TreeNode p, TreeNode q)
if (p.val < node.val && q.val < node.val) node = node.left!;
else if (p.val > node.val && q.val > node.val) node = node.right!;
else return node;          // split point: p and q diverge here

frames.caption: Find LCA(1, 4). Start at the root and walk down — in a BST the ordering tells you which direction to go.
frames.note: The LCA is the deepest node that has both targets as descendants. On a BST we never explore subtrees blindly: at each node we compare both targets against node.val. That single comparison decides left, right, or "done" — so the whole search is one straight walk from the root, O(h) time and O(1) space.

frames.caption: Visit node 5. Compare both targets (p=1, q=4) against 5 to pick a direction.
frames.note: The whole loop is this one node, this one comparison. We ask a single yes/no question — are both targets on the same side of node.val? — and the BST ordering guarantees the answer is reliable. No subtree is ever searched speculatively.

frames.caption: Both 1 < 5 and 4 < 5 → both targets are smaller → go LEFT to 3.
frames.note: Both targets are smaller than this node, so by the BST invariant both must live in the LEFT subtree (every value left of a node is strictly smaller). The common ancestor cannot be here or anywhere to the right — it has to be deeper on the left. We step left and shrink the search to that subtree, which is why each step halves the remaining height: O(h) total.

frames.caption: Visit node 3. Compare both targets (p=1, q=4) against 3 to pick a direction.
frames.note: The whole loop is this one node, this one comparison. We ask a single yes/no question — are both targets on the same side of node.val? — and the BST ordering guarantees the answer is reliable. No subtree is ever searched speculatively.

frames.caption: Not both smaller, not both larger: 1 ≤ 3 ≤ 4. p and q split here → return 3.
frames.note: The targets now fall on OPPOSITE sides of this node (one ≤ node ≤ other), so this is the first node whose value lies BETWEEN p and q. That is exactly the definition of the split point: p descends one way, q the other, so the deepest node that still has BOTH as descendants is this one. It is the LCA — stop and return it.

frames.caption: LCA(1, 4) = 3. We walked 5 → 3 — a single root-to-LCA path, O(h) time and O(1) extra space.
frames.note: Notice what we never did: no recursion, no visited set, no scanning of unrelated subtrees. The BST ordering turned an O(n) general-tree problem into a straight descent whose length is just the tree height. If this were a plain binary tree (no ordering), we would fall back to the postorder version — recurse both sides and return the node where the two found targets meet.
